duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: In _claim for non-ETH tokens, claimedAmount is set to address(this).balance (absolute) instead of the swap output delta, causing any residual ETH in the contract to be swept by the claimer
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same core accounting bug: _claim uses address(this).balance instead of measuring the swap delta (boughtETHAmount). OPS-H016 identifies the open receive() as the enabler but the exploitable defect is the same balance sweep. TECH2-H005 is the first instance of that duplicated ID (Absolute Balance Usage).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Zero or low _percentage parameter causes userClaim to round to 0 via integer division, leaving user balance unchanged while claimedAmount captures the entire contract ETH balance — enabling repeatable costless drain
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same amplification of DUP-001: missing validation on _percentage allows userClaim=0 via truncation, so balance is never reduced and the absolute-balance sweep is infinitely repeatable. TECH-H010 is a rounding variant of the same bug. ECON2-H008 describes the same via claimAndStake entry point.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: UniswapV3 calldata validation uses hardcoded assembly offsets for encodedPath, allowing non-standard ABI encoding to bypass inputToken/outputToken checks while the 0x proxy follows the real offset pointer
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same parsing defect: _decodeUniswapV3Data reads encodedPath from hardcoded offset (p+96/p+128) instead of following the ABI offset pointer, enabling fake path data to pass validation while the 0x proxy executes the real path at a different offset.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: TransformERC20 calldata validation bypasses recipient check (uninitialized variable defaults to address(0)) and completely ignores the transformations array, enabling arbitrary execution through the 0x proxy
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same validation gap: _decodeTransformERC20Data never sets recipient (defaults to address(0) passing the check), and the transformations array is completely unvalidated allowing arbitrary 0x operations. ECON-H013 frames it as bypassing forced lpETH conversion but the root cause is identical. NOTE: TECH2-H003 has a duplicate ID collision — the first instance (Unvalidated TransformERC20) is this duplicate, while the second instance (Unprotected self-transfer of lpETH) is a separate unique threat. TECH2-H003 is NOT included in threats_to_remove to preserve the unique second instance.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Emergency mode fails to enable ETH withdrawals after convertAllETH because the UseClaimInstead revert check executes unconditionally outside the emergencyMode bypass, permanently locking ETH depositors if lpETH is broken
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same logic flaw in withdraw(): the check 'if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()' for ETH is nested inside the _token==ETH block but outside the emergencyMode conditional, making emergency mode ineffective for ETH depositors post-conversion. TECH2-H004 refers to the first instance of that duplicated ID (Emergency Mode Fails).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Residual ERC20 approval to the 0x exchange proxy persists after _fillQuote because the allowance is never reset to 0, enabling potential token theft via crafted subsequent claims
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same missing cleanup: _fillQuote approves exchangeProxy for _amount but never calls approve(0) after the swap, leaving dangling allowances that can be exploited via calldata manipulation bypasses. TECH2-H006 refers to the first instance of that duplicated ID (Residual Token Approvals).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Fee-on-transfer tokens cause accounting insolvency because _processLock credits balances with the requested _amount rather than the actual received amount, making the contract insolvent for that token
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same accounting mismatch: safeTransferFrom(_amount) followed by balances += _amount without balance-delta check. For fee-on-transfer tokens, contract holds less than credited, creating a bank-run where late users' transactions revert. TECH2-H007 refers to the first instance of that duplicated ID (Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Break).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Raw require(_sellToken.approve()) in _fillQuote instead of SafeERC20 causes permanent DoS for non-standard tokens that don't return bool or require zero-allowance-first semantics
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same code defect: _fillQuote uses raw approve() with require() instead of SafeERC20.safeApprove/forceApprove. Tokens without bool return (ABI decode revert) or requiring zero-first allowance (USDT-like) cause permanent claim failures. ECON-H016 and OPS-H015 focus on the USDT-style angle but the root cause and fix are identical. TECH2-H006 refers to the second instance of that duplicated ID (Non-standard approve handling).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: convertAllETH uses absolute balances (address(this).balance for ETH and lpETH.balanceOf for lpETH) instead of deltas, allowing inflation via unsolicited ETH deposits or lpETH donations before conversion
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All target the same function (convertAllETH) and same pattern: totalBalance = address(this).balance includes unsolicited ETH; totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(this) includes donated lpETH. ECON-H009 focuses on the ETH injection vector, ECON-H007 on lpETH donation, and OPS-H009 covers both — but the root cause is identical absolute-balance accounting in convertAllETH.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: setOwner allows setting owner to address(0) without validation, permanently bricking all onlyAuthorized admin functions including emergency mode
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same missing validation: setOwner has no require(_owner != address(0)) check, allowing permanent admin lockout. TECH2-H007 refers to the second instance of that duplicated ID (Owner can set owner=address(0)).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: lockETHFor and lockFor allow permissionless deposits on behalf of arbitrary addresses without recipient consent, enabling dust griefing, referral pollution, and forced balance entries
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All describe the same lack of recipient consent in lockETHFor/lockFor. TECH-H013 is the ERC20 variant of the same issue. ECON2-H011 describes depositing to address(0)/non-interactive addresses as a specific case. OPS2-H014 combines both. TECH2-H004 and TECH2-H005 are the second instances of those duplicated IDs (lockETHFor/lockFor griefing).
